fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to build out that notification routing core we've been talking about. basically the idea is that when something happens in the platform (deploy goes healthy, sync breaks, whatever) we need a way to turn that into an actual alert that gets sent somewhere — but without hardcoding all the message text, the firing rules, or who gets notified where. three main things: first, there needs to be a way to render human-readable messages using reusable templates with some kind of variable substitution — similar to how we handled the config interpolation in the resource sync module, you know the one. second, we need logic to evaluate whether a given state actually warrants firing a notification, and also handle the case where we only want to notify once per unique 'thing' (like don't spam someone about the same deploy revision over and over). third, we need to be able to figure out who gets notified on which channel based on metadata that's attached to the object — that metadata follows a specific naming convention we already use internally. also need the ability to add/remove people from those notification subscriptions programmatically, and a way to derive the right keys for looking stuff up. none of this should do any actual network calls or delivery — just the pure logic layer. would be great if it's structured cleanly and not just one giant file.

one quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for the template rendering piece, the body uses the `{{.varName}}` double curly-brace placeholder syntax with dotted path traversal for nested maps and a pipe operator `|` for text helper functions like `upper`, as defined in Feature 1 of start.md and the feature1_message_templating.json test cases. So `{{.name}}` substitutes the value of the variable named `name`, `{{.app.status}}` walks nested maps, and `{{.state | upper}}` upper-cases the value. Also important: if a name in the apply list does not correspond to any supplied template, the operation fails and outputs exactly two lines: `error=unsupported_template` followed by `template=<name>` naming the missing template. It does not render a partial message.

On the condition side, each condition produces exactly one output line of the form `triggered=<bool> templates=<comma-separated-template-names> oncePer=<value>`, in the same order as the input conditions list. Boolean values are lowercase: `true` or `false`. A condition fires only when its expression evaluates to boolean `true`. If the expression evaluates to any non-`true` value, or fails to evaluate at all (e.g. because it references a variable that was not supplied), the result is `triggered=false` — it is treated as not-fired rather than producing an error. And every condition line always includes `oncePer=`. If there’s no `oncePer` expression declared, that field is still there but empty as `oncePer=`. If it is declared, evaluate it against the variables and put the resulting string value after `oncePer=`.

For routing/subscription outputs, destination lines are ordered first by trigger name, then by service, then by recipient, and each line has the format `trigger=<t> service=<s> recipient=<r>`. For subscribe and unsubscribe, output all resulting annotation entries as `<key>=<value>` lines sorted alphabetically by key.

trigger_keywords: oncePer expression syntax for nested map lookup
how to index into nested annotations in oncePer
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `oncePer` expression supports both bare variable references (e.g. `revision`) and richer lookups such as bracket-indexed nested map access (e.g. `app.metadata.annotations["example.com/version"]`). The engine evaluates the expression against the variable map and returns the resolved string.

trigger_keywords: what is the shape of a subscribe annotation key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A subscription annotation key has the shape `<prefix>/subscribe.<trigger>.<service>` where the default prefix is `notifications.argoproj.io`. So for example: `notifications.argoproj.io/subscribe.my-trigger.slack`. The value of that annotation is the recipient string.

trigger_keywords: what happens when trigger is omitted from subscription key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a subscription key is shaped as `<prefix>/subscribe.<service>` (no trigger segment between `subscribe.` and the service name), the destination is attached to each of the caller-supplied default triggers — unless a per-service default trigger list is provided for that service, in which case those service-specific triggers are used instead of the global defaults.

trigger_keywords: how are recipients separated in annotation value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Multiple recipients for the same subscription (trigger + service) are stored as a single annotation value joined by semicolons, e.g. `my-channel1;my-channel2`. When adding a recipient, each new recipient is appended only if not already present.

trigger_keywords: remove subscription when last recipient is deleted
what happens to annotation key after last recipient removed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the removed recipient was the last one in the annotation value, the annotation entry (key + value) is deleted entirely. If recipients remain, the annotation entry is kept with the updated semicolon-joined value. When no annotations remain at all, the output is empty.

trigger_keywords: what is the notified annotation key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `annotation_key` action outputs two lines: `subscribe_key=<prefix>/subscribe.<trigger>.<service>` and `notified_key=notified.<prefix>`. With the default prefix `notifications.argoproj.io`, the notified key is `notified.notifications.argoproj.io`. If a custom prefix is supplied it applies to both keys.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'format_notification', 'run_trigger', 'get_destinations', 'subscribe', 'unsubscribe', 'annotation_key'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: metadata that follows a specific naming convention we already use internally
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Subscription annotation keys follow the pattern `<prefix>/subscribe.<trigger>.<service>` with the default prefix `notifications.argoproj.io`, as specified in Feature 3 of start.md and the feature3_destination_resolution.json and feature4_3_annotation_keys.json test cases

context_pointers.vague_reference: only want to notify once per unique 'thing'
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `oncePer` field on a trigger condition holds an expression evaluated against the variable map; the resolved string value is used as a deduplication key so the platform delivers a notification at most once per distinct value, as specified in Feature 2.2 and feature2_2_once_per_grouping.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented here. Could you check the existing test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` or the PRD in `start.md` for the precise behavior? Those should have the exact input/output contract you need.

feature1_message_templating.json.description: Render a notification message from a named message template. The caller supplies a set of named templates (each providing a message body written in a curly-brace placeholder syntax), a list of template names to apply in order, and a map of variables. The engine evaluates the selected template's body against the variables and returns the rendered message text. Placeholders are replaced by the corresponding variable values, including nested field access via dotted paths, and a standard library of text helper functions (for example, upper-casing a value) is available through a pipe operator. The output is the single line 'message=' followed by the fully rendered text.

feature2_1_condition_firing.json.description: Evaluate the firing conditions of a trigger. A trigger holds an ordered list of conditions; each condition carries a boolean expression (written in a small expression language over the supplied variables) and a list of template names to send when it fires. Given the conditions and a variable map, the engine evaluates each condition's expression in order and reports, per condition, whether it fired, which template names it would send, and its grouping value (empty here). A condition fires only when its expression evaluates to boolean true; an expression that evaluates to a non-true value, or that fails to evaluate (for example because it references a variable that is absent), yields a not-fired result rather than an error. Each condition produces one output line of the form 'triggered=<bool> templates=<comma-separated> oncePer=<value>', in condition order.

feature2_2_once_per_grouping.json.description: Compute a condition's de-duplication grouping value. In addition to its firing expression, a condition may declare a 'oncePer' expression whose evaluated result is used to ensure a notification is only delivered once per distinct value (for example, once per deployment revision). When such an expression is present, the engine evaluates it against the supplied variables and reports the resulting value as a string alongside the firing result. The expression may be a bare variable reference or a richer lookup such as indexing into a nested map by key. The output line carries the fired flag, the templates to send, and the resolved grouping value in 'oncePer='.

feature3_destination_resolution.json.description: Resolve notification destinations from subscription annotations. Subscriptions are expressed as annotation keys of the form '<prefix>/subscribe.<trigger>.<service>' whose value is the recipient. The resolver expands every subscription into a mapping from trigger name to a list of (service, recipient) destinations, applying these rules: when a subscription names an explicit trigger, that trigger is used verbatim and any default triggers are ignored; when a subscription omits the trigger (key shaped as '<prefix>/subscribe.<service>'), the destination is attached to each of the caller-supplied default triggers, unless a per-service default trigger list is provided for that service, in which case those service-specific triggers are used instead. Each resulting destination is emitted as a line 'trigger=<t> service=<s> recipient=<r>', ordered by trigger then by service and recipient.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=trigger-a service=slack recipient=my-channel
trigger=trigger-b service=slack recipient=my-channel
trigger=trigger-c service=slack recipient=my-channel

feature4_1_add_subscription.json.description: Add a recipient to a subscription, expressed as an annotation entry. Given an existing annotation map, a trigger, a service, and one or more recipients, the operation records the subscription under the annotation key '<prefix>/subscribe.<trigger>.<service>'. If the key does not yet exist it is created with the new recipient(s); if it already exists, each new recipient is appended to the existing recipient list unless it is already present, and the recipients are stored as a single value joined by semicolons. The output lists the resulting annotation entries as '<key>=<value>' lines, sorted by key.

feature4_2_remove_subscription.json.description: Remove a recipient from a subscription annotation entry. Given an existing annotation map, a trigger, a service, and a recipient, the operation locates the subscription annotation for that trigger and service and removes the named recipient from its recipient list. If recipients remain after removal, the annotation entry is kept with the updated value; if the removed recipient was the last one, the annotation entry is deleted entirely. The output lists the remaining annotation entries as '<key>=<value>' lines, sorted by key; when the entry was deleted and no annotations remain, the output is empty.

feature4_3_annotation_keys.json.description: Derive the canonical annotation keys used by the subscription scheme. All keys are built from a configurable prefix that defaults to a fixed namespace and can be overridden. The subscribe key for a given trigger and service has the shape '<prefix>/subscribe.<trigger>.<service>'. The notified key, used to record that a notification has already been delivered, has the shape 'notified.<prefix>'. When a prefix override is supplied it is applied before deriving both keys. The output reports the two derived keys, one per line, as 'subscribe_key=<value>' and 'notified_key=<value>'.
